headline: The listener leans in when the voice stops performing.
dek: BrainDiff treats each file as a listening path: phrase by phrase, pause by pause, with the neural shifts that matter for attention, trust, and recall.

a.label: A
a.name: calm_read.wav
a.summary: A steady read with even pacing and a softer close.

transcript.text: Most teams know their message is not landing.
They test clicks, but they rarely test what the brain is actually doing.
BrainDiff compares two versions and shows the cortical response.
So you can choose the message people remember.

b.label: B
b.name: intimate_read.wav
b.summary: A closer voice, stronger pauses, and a more direct final line.

transcript.text: But the brain may be ignoring the part you care about.
BrainDiff shows where each version pulls attention, trust, and memory.
Then it tells you which version deserves to ship.

moments.title: The pause does work
moments.detail: B pauses before the problem statement. Attention rises because the listener gets a clean beat to anticipate the point.
moments.move: Protect the pause before the warning line.

moments.title: Specific promise beats generic clarity
moments.detail: Trust climbs when B names attention, trust, and memory instead of saying cortical response.
moments.move: Use concrete listener outcomes in the main claim.

moments.title: The final line sticks
moments.detail: The sharper shipping language creates the strongest memory lock in the run.
moments.move: Keep the direct close, but trim the breath before it.

recommendations: Ship B's voice direction and final line.
Keep A's steadier opening volume if the channel is noisy.
The useful contrast is not loudness. It is pacing, specificity, and the last sentence.
